Category: --- PROJECT LEADERSHIP: executives and managers who run the project ---

Description: Owner's on-site or dedicated agent who makes decisions on the owner's behalf — design review, scope decisions, change authorization, acceptance of work, dispute resolution, stakeholder management. Consumes budget reports, schedule updates, change requests, pay applications. Produces change authorizations, design approvals, acceptance letters, field directives. Active across all phases from conception through warranty. Decision authority: High — authorized to make binding decisions for owner on scope, cost, and schedule.
Industry Standard: CMAA (owner representation standards); AIA A201 (owner's role); PMI PMP
Common Values: Owner's rep; OR; owner's PM; client representative
Source Systems: Procore (project_users, project_role_memberships); Primavera Unifier; Kahua; e-Builder; Excel
Procore Coverage: Medium — tracked in project_role_memberships and project_users; role_name may include Owner's Rep but not consistently classified
Benchmarking Use: Compare owner's rep decision response times and change authorization turnaround across project types
Notes: Scope of authority varies by contract — some can authorize changes up to a threshold, others must escalate. Critical gatekeeper. Cross-domain: Organizations (Owner/Developer), Financial Instruments (change authorization).

Description: Senior leader overseeing a project or group of projects for the GC or CM — portfolio oversight, client relationship, risk escalation, financial performance, resource allocation, contract negotiations. Consumes monthly financial reports, schedule summaries, risk registers. Produces executive summaries, escalation decisions, resource commitments. Active across all phases with higher involvement during procurement and major milestones. Decision authority: High — approves GMP, major change orders, staffing.
Industry Standard: PMI PMP/PgMP; AGC project executive standards
Common Values: Project exec; PE; VP of operations; division manager
Source Systems: Procore (project_users); ERP systems (Sage, Viewpoint, CMiC); Excel
Procore Coverage: Low — tracked in project_users but no structured PE role classification; PE activity not captured in daily workflows
Benchmarking Use: Compare PE portfolio span (projects per PE) and escalation frequency across company sizes
Notes: Typically manages 2-5 projects simultaneously. The PE is the GC's senior relationship with the owner. Gets involved for major decisions and problem resolution.

Description: Day-to-day leader of the project — owns schedule, budget, and subcontractor management. Consumes daily reports, budget/schedule updates, RFIs, submittals, change events, pay applications. Produces monthly reports, budget forecasts, schedule updates, meeting minutes, change order packages. Active from Procurement through Closeout. Decision authority: Medium-High — manages budget and schedule; recommends changes; escalates major issues to PE.
Industry Standard: PMI PMP; CMAA CCM; AGC project management standards
Common Values: PM; project manager; construction manager (on owner side)
Source Systems: Procore (primary user — all modules); Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; MS Project; P6; Excel
Procore Coverage: High — project_role_memberships tracks PM role (role_name = 'Project Manager'); PM is the primary Procore power user across all modules
Benchmarking Use: Compare PM workload (projects per PM) and project performance (cost growth
Notes: schedule variance) across project types
column10: The central hub of project information — PM touches every UCDM domain. GC PM vs. owner PM have different perspectives but both need the same data. Cross-domain: all domains.

Description: Supports PM with day-to-day management — often assigned specific scope areas or administrative functions. Manages submittal tracking, RFI coordination, subcontract administration, insurance tracking, meeting coordination, document control. Active from Procurement through Closeout. Decision authority: Low-Medium — executes PM decisions; manages defined scope areas; limited authorization for minor changes.
Industry Standard: PMI CAPM; AGC; CMAA
Common Values: APM; assistant PM; junior PM
Source Systems: Procore (submittals, RFIs, commitments, correspondence, document management); Sage 300; Viewpoint; Excel
Procore Coverage: Medium — tracked in project_users; may have project_role_memberships entry; APM is a heavy Procore user but role not always distinguished from PM
Benchmarking Use: Compare APM scope coverage (trades managed per APM) and administrative task completion rates
Notes: Career progression: APM → PM → PE. The APM role is where most people learn construction management. Typically assigned specific trades. Cross-domain: Documents (submittals, RFIs), Financial Instruments (subcontract admin).

Description: Manages the design process and design team coordination for the GC or owner — design review, constructability review, design schedule management, value engineering. Primarily a design-build or CM at Risk role. Active during Preconstruction; extends into Construction for design-build. Decision authority: Medium — recommends design decisions; manages design consultants; does not stamp drawings.
Industry Standard: AIA (design phase standards); DBIA (design-build management); AGC
Common Values: Design manager; VDC director; preconstruction design lead
Source Systems: Procore (RFIs, submittals, drawings, coordination issues); BIM 360; Revit; Bluebeam; Newforma
Procore Coverage: Low — not a standard project_role_memberships role; design manager activity captured indirectly through RFI/submittal workflows
Benchmarking Use: Compare design review turnaround times and constructability issue identification rates across delivery methods
Notes: Bridges the gap between design intent and construction reality. Manages the A/E team on behalf of the GC. Cross-domain: Organizations (Design Team), Phases (Design Development).

Category: --- FIELD OPERATIONS: personnel who manage work on the jobsite ---

Description: Senior field leader responsible for day-to-day construction operations — trade coordination, schedule enforcement, quality oversight, safety enforcement, site logistics, daily reporting. Consumes lookaheads, daily logs, inspection schedules, safety reports, coordination notes, drawing sets. Produces daily reports, coordination schedules, field directives, site logistics plans. Active from Construction through Closeout. Decision authority: Medium-High — controls site access, work sequence, schedule priorities; can stop unsafe work.
Industry Standard: OSHA 29 CFR 1926 (competent person requirements); AGC (field management)
Common Values: Superintendent; super; site manager; general superintendent
Source Systems: Procore (daily logs — primary creator, inspections, observations, punch items, schedule); Raken; Fieldwire; PlanGrid
Procore Coverage: High — project_role_memberships tracks superintendent role; super is the primary daily log creator and field Procore user
Benchmarking Use: Compare superintendent span of control (subcontractors per super) and daily reporting compliance rates
Notes: The superintendent owns the field — everything on site flows through the super. Typically the most experienced person on the project. Cross-domain: Field Operations (daily logs), Phases (all construction phases).

Description: Supports superintendent with field coordination — often assigned to specific building areas or trades. Manages area-specific trade coordination, daily documentation, inspection coordination, material tracking, quality walks. Active from Construction through Closeout. Decision authority: Low-Medium — manages assigned area; escalates issues to superintendent.
Industry Standard: OSHA 29 CFR 1926; AGC
Common Values: Assistant super; area super; assistant superintendent
Source Systems: Procore (daily logs, inspections, observations, punch items); Raken; Fieldwire; PlanGrid
Procore Coverage: Medium — tracked in project_users; may appear in project_role_memberships; creates daily logs and field records for assigned areas
Benchmarking Use: Compare assistant superintendent area assignments and deficiency identification rates per area
Notes: Often assigned specific floors, areas, or trade groups. Career path to superintendent. May have 1-3 areas depending on project size.

Description: Technical field support — manages engineering and documentation aspects of field work. Drafts RFIs, coordinates submittals, tracks as-builts, reviews shop drawings, tracks quantities, solves technical problems. Bridge between office (PM team) and field (super team). Active from Construction through Closeout. Decision authority: Low — identifies issues and proposes solutions; does not authorize changes.
Industry Standard: PMI; AGC; AACE (project controls)
Common Values: PE (field); project engineer; field office engineer
Source Systems: Procore (RFIs, submittals, daily logs, as-built markups); Bluebeam; Excel; BIM tools
Procore Coverage: Medium — tracked in project_users; creates RFIs, manages submittals in Procore; role not always distinguished in project_role_memberships
Benchmarking Use: Compare project engineer RFI drafting rates and submittal processing turnaround across project types
Notes: Often the first to identify technical conflicts. Strong career development role. Cross-domain: Documents (RFIs, submittals), Building Elements (technical issues).

Description: Entry-level field position focused on surveying, layout, and construction documentation — concrete placement monitoring, quantity verification, material receiving, photo documentation. Starting point for most construction careers. Active during Construction. Decision authority: Low — executes technical tasks under supervision; flags discrepancies.
Industry Standard: OSHA 10/30 (safety training); survey instrument certifications
Common Values: Field engineer; junior engineer; layout engineer
Source Systems: Procore (daily logs, photos, material receiving); survey instruments (Trimble, Topcon, Leica); Bluebeam
Procore Coverage: Low — tracked in project_users but field engineer role rarely classified in project_role_memberships; photo and daily log contributions tracked
Benchmarking Use: Compare field engineer documentation volume and discrepancy identification rates across project types
Notes: Gets hands dirty — learns how buildings actually go together. Transitions to PE or assistant super. Cross-domain: Field Operations (daily documentation).

Description: Trade-specific field leader who manages a crew of 4-20 workers on a daily basis — daily work assignments, material coordination, quality of installed work, safety compliance, productivity. The link between management and craft workers. Active from Construction through Closeout. Decision authority: Low — manages own crew's daily work; escalates to super or PM.
Industry Standard: OSHA 29 CFR 1926 (competent person); union agreements (foreman classification); trade-specific certifications
Common Values: Foreman; general foreman; GF; lead hand; working foreman
Source Systems: Procore (daily logs — may submit for crew, T&M tickets); timekeeping systems; subcontractor field apps
Procore Coverage: Medium — daily_log_construction_reports tracks foreman_hours; daily_log_manpower captures crew-level data; foreman not individually classified
Benchmarking Use: Compare foreman crew sizes and crew productivity rates across trades and project types
Notes: A good foreman maximizes crew productivity. Cross-domain: Resources (Trades), Field Operations (manpower tracking).

Description: Skilled tradesperson who performs the physical construction work — licensed or certified in their trade. Installs trade-specific work per drawings and specs, maintains quality workmanship, complies with safety procedures. The people who actually build the building. Active during Construction. Decision authority: None — executes work as directed; reports unsafe conditions.
Industry Standard: State licensing (electricians, plumbers); OSHA 10 (safety); union journeyman certification; trade-specific certifications
Common Values: Journeyman; mechanic; tradesman; craft worker; skilled laborer
Source Systems: Procore (daily logs — reported by foreman/super); timekeeping systems; union dispatch systems
Procore Coverage: Medium — daily_log_construction_reports tracks journeyman_hours and headcounts; daily_log_manpower captures individual or crew-level data
Benchmarking Use: Compare journeyman headcount trends and labor productivity across trades and project phases
Notes: Licensed electricians, plumbers, ironworkers, operators, etc. Union or non-union. Apprenticeship programs feed the pipeline. Cross-domain: Resources (Trades).

Description: Trades worker in training — learning under journeyman supervision as part of a formal 3-5 year program. Performs assigned tasks, attends trade school, progresses toward journeyman status. Active during Construction. Decision authority: None — works under direct journeyman supervision. Apprentice-to-journeyman ratio is regulated.
Industry Standard: DOL (Department of Labor — registered apprenticeship); state apprenticeship standards; union apprenticeship programs
Common Values: Apprentice; first-year through fourth-year; indentured apprentice
Source Systems: Procore (daily logs — reported as crew); timekeeping systems; apprenticeship management systems; union training centers
Procore Coverage: Medium — daily_log_construction_reports tracks apprentice_hours separately; headcount captured in manpower logs
Benchmarking Use: Compare apprentice utilization rates and apprentice-to-journeyman ratios across trades and project types
Notes: Formal apprenticeship programs are the primary skilled labor pipeline. Required on many public projects with workforce development mandates. Cross-domain: Resources (Trades).

Category: --- SAFETY & QUALITY: personnel responsible for safety compliance and quality assurance ---

Description: Dedicated safety professional responsible for the project's or company's safety program — site inspections, incident investigation, OSHA compliance, safety training, subcontractor safety oversight. Consumes OSHA regulations, safety plans, incident reports, observation reports, EMR data. Produces safety metrics (TRIR, DART, EMR), corrective actions, training records. Active primarily during Construction; extends into Preconstruction for planning. Decision authority: Medium — can stop unsafe work immediately; recommends corrective actions.
Industry Standard: OSHA 29 CFR 1926; ANSI Z10 (safety management); BCSP CSP/CHST certifications
Common Values: Safety manager; safety director; HSE manager; EHS director
Source Systems: Procore (observations — safety, daily log safety violations, incidents); Safety Reports; iAuditor; SiteDocs; Predictive Solutions
Procore Coverage: Medium — project_role_memberships tracks Safety Manager role (confirmed in data mapping); safety observations and incidents capture activity
Benchmarking Use: Compare safety manager-to-worker ratios and TRIR outcomes across project types and sizes
Notes: On large projects, full-time dedicated. On smaller projects, superintendent may double as safety. Third-party consultants common. Cross-domain: Quality & Safety Records, Recurring Violations (insight #11).

Description: Field-level safety personnel conducting daily site safety activities — safety walks, toolbox talks, new worker orientation, PPE compliance, housekeeping inspections, near-miss documentation. Boots-on-the-ground safety presence. Active during Construction. Decision authority: Low-Medium — can stop unsafe work; reports to safety manager.
Industry Standard: OSHA 29 CFR 1926; BCSP OHST/STS certifications; OSHA 30-hour
Common Values: Safety officer; safety coordinator; site safety; safety tech
Source Systems: Procore (observations — safety, daily logs); safety management apps; inspection checklists
Procore Coverage: Low — may be tracked in project_users but no dedicated safety officer role in project_role_memberships; activity captured through safety observations
Benchmarking Use: Compare safety observation volume per safety officer and near-miss reporting rates across projects
Notes: Multiple safety officers on large projects. Subcontractor safety officers supplement GC staff. Cross-domain: Quality & Safety Records (observations).

Description: Dedicated quality professional responsible for QA/QC across the project — inspection coordination, deficiency tracking, specification compliance, material testing coordination, non-conformance management. Consumes specifications, approved submittals, test reports, inspection results. Produces inspection reports, NCRs, deficiency logs, quality metrics. Active from Construction through Closeout. Decision authority: Medium — can reject non-conforming work; requires corrective action.
Industry Standard: ISO 9001 (quality management); ASQ CQM/CQE certifications; project-specific quality plans
Common Values: QC manager; quality manager; QA/QC inspector; quality engineer
Source Systems: Procore (inspections, observations — quality, punch items); quality management systems; Bluebeam
Procore Coverage: Medium — inspections and observations track QC activity; project_role_memberships may include QC roles; no dedicated quality management workflow
Benchmarking Use: Compare quality inspection pass rates and NCR frequency across project types and complexity levels
Notes: Dedicated QC roles more common on large/complex projects (healthcare, data centers, federal). On typical commercial, quality falls to super and PE. Cross-domain: Quality & Safety Records.

Category: --- PRECONSTRUCTION & ESTIMATING: personnel who plan the project before construction ---

Description: Develops cost estimates from conceptual through detailed pricing — quantity takeoff, unit pricing, subcontractor bid solicitation and leveling, risk pricing, GMP development, value engineering. Consumes drawings, specifications, bid documents, historical cost data, subcontractor bids. Produces cost estimates, bid proposals, GMP packages, VE logs, bid tabulations. Active during Preconstruction. Decision authority: Medium — cost opinions inform major decisions; recommends contingency levels.
Industry Standard: AACE CEP (certified estimating professional); ASPE (American Society of Professional Estimators)
Common Values: Estimator; chief estimator; senior estimator; cost estimator
Source Systems: Procore (Estimating, bidding); On-Screen Takeoff; Planswift; HCSS HeavyBid; Sage Estimating; ProEst; Excel
Procore Coverage: Medium — Procore Estimating tables exist (estimates, estimate_layers); bidding module supports bid solicitation; adoption growing
Benchmarking Use: Compare estimate accuracy (estimate vs. actual) and bid hit rates across project types and estimators
Notes: Estimating is where projects are won or lost. Accuracy depends on experience and historical data. Conceptual ±20%, detailed ±5%. Cross-domain: Financial Instruments (budget origination).

Description: Leads the preconstruction effort — coordinates estimating, scheduling, constructability, client presentations, GMP negotiation, value engineering facilitation, risk assessment, subcontractor prequalification. Active during Preconstruction. Decision authority: Medium-High — leads GMP development; recommends scope and budget decisions; negotiates with owner.
Industry Standard: CMAA; DBIA (design-build preconstruction); AGC CM/GC preconstruction standards
Common Values: Precon manager; preconstruction director; BD/precon lead
Source Systems: Procore (Estimating, bidding, schedule); Building Connected (prequalification); P6; MS Project; Excel
Procore Coverage: Low — no dedicated preconstruction manager role in project_role_memberships; precon activity spans estimating and bidding modules
Benchmarking Use: Compare GMP development timelines and win rates across delivery methods and project types
Notes: The GC's lead during the pre-award phase. Win rate depends on ability to build trust with owner and architect. Cross-domain: Organizations (CM at Risk), Phases (Preconstruction).

Category: --- PROJECT CONTROLS: personnel who manage schedule, cost, and risk data ---

Description: Manages the project schedule and schedule-related analytics — schedule development, updates, critical path analysis, delay analysis, resource loading, earned value management, schedule risk assessment. Active across all phases from preconstruction through closeout. Decision authority: Medium — schedule analysis informs decisions but does not authorize them; provides forensic basis for claims.
Industry Standard: AACE PSP (planning and scheduling professional); PMI-SP (scheduling professional); Guild of Project Controls
Common Values: Scheduler; planner; project controls manager; planning engineer
Source Systems: Procore (schedule_task, schedule_lookahead); Oracle P6; MS Project; Asta Powerproject; Primavera Risk Analysis; Deltek
Procore Coverage: Medium — schedule_task and schedule_lookahead tables exist; schedule_task_changes tracks deltas; but no resource loading or earned value
Benchmarking Use: Compare schedule update frequency and critical path stability across project types and scheduling tools
Notes: Schedule management ranges from basic bar charts (small) to full P6 with EVM (large). Delay analysis expertise is critical for claims. Cross-domain: Schedule (all elements), Phases (milestone tracking).

Description: Manages the project document management system — document numbering, drawing distribution, revision control, submittal routing, RFI routing, transmittal management, archiving. Active across all phases. Decision authority: Low — manages process and routing; does not make content decisions.
Industry Standard: CSI (document management standards); ISO 9001 (document control); AIA (document numbering)
Common Values: Document controller; DC; document manager; records manager
Source Systems: Procore (documents, drawings, submittals, RFIs, transmittals); Aconex; ProjectWise; Newforma; SharePoint; BIM 360 Docs
Procore Coverage: Medium — Procore document management, drawing revision tracking, and transmittal modules support DC workflows; no dedicated DC role tracking
Benchmarking Use: Compare document control compliance rates and revision distribution timeliness across project types
Notes: On large projects, a full-time role. On smaller projects, the APM handles it. Discipline (or lack thereof) affects every other role. Cross-domain: Documents & Communications (all elements).

Description: Tracks and forecasts project costs — cost tracking, forecast updates, change order impact analysis, cash flow projections, earned value analysis, budget variance reporting. More common on large projects ($50M+). Active from Procurement through Closeout. Decision authority: Low-Medium — provides financial analysis; recommends cost actions; does not authorize expenditures.
Industry Standard: AACE CCE (certified cost engineer); AACE EVP; PMI PMP (cost management)
Common Values: Cost engineer; cost analyst; project controls analyst; financial analyst (project)
Source Systems: Procore (budget, commitments, change orders, pay applications); Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Excel; EcoSys
Procore Coverage: Medium — Procore financial modules (budget, commitments, change orders, pay apps) provide cost tracking data; no native earned value or forecasting
Benchmarking Use: Compare cost forecast accuracy and budget variance trends across project types and cost engineer involvement
Notes: Cost engineering and scheduling increasingly converge in project controls. On smaller projects, the PM handles cost management directly. Cross-domain: Financial Instruments (all cost surfaces).

Category: --- BIM & TECHNOLOGY: personnel managing digital models and technology ---

Description: Manages the building information model and virtual design and construction processes — BIM execution plan, model management, clash detection, trade coordination, 4D scheduling, quantity extraction, reality capture management. Bridges design and construction. Active from Preconstruction through Closeout. Decision authority: Medium — drives coordination decisions; model is the authority for spatial coordination.
Industry Standard: AIA E203 (BIM protocol); BIMForum LOD Spec; AGC BIM education; buildingSMART (IFC/openBIM)
Common Values: BIM manager; VDC coordinator; VDC manager; BIM lead; digital construction manager
Source Systems: Procore (coordination issues, models, drawings); Navisworks; BIM 360; Revit; Trimble Connect; Synchro
Procore Coverage: Medium — coordination_issue table captures BIM-driven coordination; models can be linked; but no native BIM model management in Procore
Benchmarking Use: Compare clash detection rates and coordination issue resolution times across project sizes and BIM maturity levels
Notes: BIM/VDC roles are standard on projects over $20M. LOD specification governs model reliability. Cross-domain: Phases (Above-Ceiling Coordination), Building Elements (all systems).

Description: Manages project technology infrastructure — software deployment, mobile device management, network infrastructure, system integration, data management, user training. Increasingly important as construction becomes more technology-dependent. Active across all phases. Decision authority: Low — enables technology; does not make construction decisions.
Industry Standard: CompTIA; ITIL; construction-specific IT certifications
Common Values: IT manager; technology director; construction technology manager
Source Systems: Procore (administration, integrations); enterprise IT systems; MDM platforms; cloud services
Procore Coverage: Low — Procore admin and integration settings; technology manager role not tracked in project_role_memberships
Benchmarking Use: Compare technology adoption rates and system utilization across project types and company sizes
Notes: Field tablets, drones, 360 cameras, IoT sensors all require technology management. Cross-domain: all domains (technology enables data capture across every domain).

Category: --- PROCUREMENT & CONTRACTS: personnel managing commercial relationships ---

Description: Manages the procurement process from bid solicitation through contract execution — bid package development, bid solicitation, bid leveling, scope review, contract negotiation, insurance and bonding verification. Active from Preconstruction through Procurement; extends into Construction for POs. Decision authority: Medium — recommends awards; negotiates contracts; does not authorize commitments without PM/PE approval.
Industry Standard: ISM (Institute for Supply Management); AGC (procurement standards)
Common Values: Procurement manager; buyout manager; purchasing manager
Source Systems: Procore (bidding, commitments, vendors, insurance); Building Connected; SmartBid; PlanHub; Excel
Procore Coverage: Medium — bidding and commitment modules support procurement workflows; vendor prequalification available; bid tables not in analytics gold layer
Benchmarking Use: Compare buyout completion timelines and bid coverage rates across project sizes and delivery methods
Notes: On large projects, a dedicated role. On smaller projects, PM handles procurement. Early procurement of long-lead items is schedule-critical. Cross-domain: Organizations (Trade Contractors), Financial Instruments (Commitments).

Description: Manages contract compliance and commercial processes after execution — change order processing, pay application review, lien waiver collection, insurance certificate tracking, retainage management. Active from Construction through Closeout. Decision authority: Low-Medium — processes commercial transactions; ensures compliance; does not authorize scope or cost changes.
Industry Standard: AIA A201 (contract administration); CMAA; state lien law
Common Values: Contract admin; CA; commercial manager; contracts manager
Source Systems: Procore (commitments, change orders, pay applications, insurance, lien waivers); Sage 300; Viewpoint; Textura; CMiC
Procore Coverage: Medium — Procore financial modules (commitments, change orders, pay apps, insurance tracking) support contract admin workflows
Benchmarking Use: Compare change order processing times and lien waiver collection completeness rates across project types
Notes: Often combined with APM role on mid-size projects. The commercial backbone of the project. Cross-domain: Financial Instruments (all commercial instruments), Organizations (Trade Contractors).

Category: --- COMMISSIONING & STARTUP: personnel managing systems verification ---

Description: Independent professional who verifies building systems perform as designed — commissioning plan development, pre-functional testing, functional performance testing, deficiency tracking, seasonal testing, final report. Not part of design or construction team. Active from Construction through Post-Construction (seasonal). Decision authority: Medium — identifies deficiencies and requires correction; does not authorize design changes or additional cost.
Industry Standard: ASHRAE Guideline 0; ASHRAE 202; LEED EAp1; BCxA certification (BCA); ACG certification
Common Values: CxA; commissioning agent; commissioning authority; Cx manager
Source Systems: Procore (observations, inspections); Cx Alloy; FacilityONE; CxPlanner; BAS systems
Procore Coverage: Low — observations and inspections can informally track CxA findings; no dedicated commissioning workflow or role classification
Benchmarking Use: Compare commissioning deficiency rates and seasonal test completion timelines across building types
Notes: CxA independence is key — represents owner's interest in system performance. LEED requires CxA. Enhanced CxA extends to envelope. Cross-domain: Organizations (CxA firm), Phases (Commissioning).

Description: Testing, Adjusting, and Balancing technician who measures and adjusts air and water flow rates to design values — air system balancing, hydronic balancing, sound and vibration measurement. TAB firm independence from MEP contractor is important for objectivity. Active during Commissioning phase. Decision authority: Low — measures and adjusts to design values; reports deficiencies; does not redesign systems.
Industry Standard: AABC (certification); NEBB (certification); ASHRAE 111 (testing and balancing)
Common Values: TAB technician; TAB engineer; air balance technician; water balance technician
Source Systems: TAB instruments (flow hoods, manometers, balometers); BAS systems; Procore (observations — informal)
Procore Coverage: None — TAB activity not tracked in Procore; TAB reports stored as documents only
Benchmarking Use: Compare TAB deficiency rates and re-balancing frequency across building types and HVAC complexity
Notes: TAB data is foundational — HVAC commissioning depends on balanced systems. Results directly affect occupant comfort. Cross-domain: Phases (Testing Adjusting & Balancing), Building Elements (HVAC).

Category: --- OFFICE & ADMINISTRATIVE: personnel supporting project operations ---

Description: Manages the project office and administrative operations — office operations, filing, correspondence management, meeting scheduling, visitor management, event coordination. Active across all phases. Decision authority: Low — supports project operations; does not make construction or commercial decisions.
Industry Standard: No specific industry standard — general office management
Common Values: Office manager; project admin; project coordinator; admin assistant
Source Systems: Procore (meeting minutes, correspondence, daily log visitors); office management tools; MS Office; scheduling tools
Procore Coverage: Low — Procore meeting and correspondence modules capture admin output; no office manager role tracking
Benchmarking Use: Compare administrative support ratios (admin staff per project team size) across project sizes
Notes: On large projects, a dedicated role managing a trailer with 10-30 people. On smaller projects, admin tasks fall to APM or PE.

Description: Manages project financial transactions and reporting — invoice processing, pay application processing, job cost tracking, financial reporting, audit support, tax compliance. Company-level role supporting multiple projects. Active across all phases. Decision authority: Low — processes financial data; does not authorize expenditures (PM/PE authorize).
Industry Standard: AICPA; state CPA requirements; construction-specific accounting (percentage of completion)
Common Values: Project accountant; financial analyst; AP/AR; cost accountant; controller (project)
Source Systems: Procore (pay applications, invoices, commitments); Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Foundation; QuickBooks
Procore Coverage: Medium — Procore financial modules (pay apps, invoices, commitments) feed accounting workflows; integration with ERP systems common
Benchmarking Use: Compare invoice processing cycle times and financial reporting timeliness across project types
Notes: On large projects, may have dedicated project accountant. Financial close each month drives reporting cadence. Cross-domain: Financial Instruments (billing and payment).

Category: --- STANDARD MEASURES: KPIs and metrics derived from role performance and staffing ---

Description: Ratio of management staff (PM, super, PE, APM) to project value or subcontractor count — indicates project resourcing adequacy. Calculated as management headcount / project value band or management headcount / active subcontractor count.
Industry Standard: PMI PMBOK (resource management); AGC staffing benchmarks
Common Values: Typical: 1 PM per $20-50M; 1 super per 10-15 active subs; 1 APM per 8-12 trades managed
Source Systems: Procore (project_role_memberships, project_users); HR systems; staffing tools
Procore Coverage: High — project_role_memberships and project_users provide headcount per role per project
Benchmarking Use: Compare staffing ratios across project types and value bands to identify under/over-resourced projects
Notes: Under-staffed projects correlate with higher deficiency rates, slower RFI response, and more schedule slippage.

Description: Percentage of key project roles filled at any point in the project timeline — filled roles / required roles × 100. Tracks gaps in project team staffing that create risk.
Industry Standard: PMI PMBOK; AGC project staffing standards
Common Values: Expressed as percentage; target: 100% for core roles (PM, super, safety); typical: 85-95%
Source Systems: Procore (project_role_memberships); HR systems; staffing tools
Procore Coverage: High — project_role_memberships shows which roles are assigned per project; gaps detectable by missing role assignments
Benchmarking Use: Compare role coverage rates across project types and identify which roles are most frequently vacant
Notes: Safety manager and QC roles are the most commonly vacant on mid-size projects. Cross-domain: Organizations (staffing responsibility).

Description: Number of active projects per project manager — indicates workload balance and risk of attention dilution. Calculated from PM role assignments across the active project portfolio.
Industry Standard: PMI (resource management); CMAA (CM staffing)
Common Values: Typical: 1-2 projects (large/complex); 2-4 projects (mid-size); 3-6 projects (small)
Source Systems: Procore (project_role_memberships across projects); HR systems; portfolio tools
Procore Coverage: High — project_role_memberships enables cross-project PM assignment analysis
Benchmarking Use: Compare PM workload distribution and correlate with project performance outcomes (cost growth
Notes: schedule variance)
column10: PMs managing too many projects show degraded performance across all projects. Cross-domain: Project Attributes (project size and complexity).

Description: Number of active subcontractors and direct reports managed by a single superintendent — indicates field coordination burden. Calculated from active vendor count and crew headcount per superintendent.
Industry Standard: AGC (field management staffing); OSHA competent person requirements
Common Values: Typical: 8-15 active subs per super (manageable); 15-25 (stretched); 25+ (high risk)
Source Systems: Procore (project_vendors, daily_log_manpower, project_role_memberships); field management tools
Procore Coverage: High — project_vendors count and project_role_memberships superintendent assignments enable calculation
Benchmarking Use: Compare superintendent span of control and correlate with safety incident rates and daily log completion rates
Notes: High span of control correlates with lower daily log compliance, more safety incidents, and more punch items. Cross-domain: Field Operations, Quality & Safety Records.

Description: Ratio of dedicated safety personnel to total workforce — safety staff headcount / peak daily workforce × 100. Indicates safety program investment and correlates with incident rates.
Industry Standard: OSHA recommendations; company safety programs; owner safety requirements
Common Values: Typical: 1 safety person per 50-100 workers; owner requirements may be stricter (1:25 on some projects)
Source Systems: Procore (project_role_memberships — safety roles, daily_log_manpower — peak headcount); safety management systems
Procore Coverage: Medium — project_role_memberships tracks safety manager; daily_log_manpower provides peak workforce; but safety officer count may be incomplete
Benchmarking Use: Compare safety staffing ratios and correlate with TRIR and observation density across project types
Notes: Higher safety staffing ratios consistently correlate with lower incident rates. Cross-domain: Quality & Safety Records, Recurring Violations (insight #11).
